docs.title: Ravindra Singh
docs.text:
PhD Candidate in Geography, Jawaharlal Nehru University

Spatial justice researcher mapping who gets left out of Delhi's green city agenda

docs.title: About Me
docs.text:
Delhi is officially one of India's greenest cities. It is also a city where millions of residents in informal and unauthorised settlements live without meaningful access to green space, cooling shade, or clean urban environments. This paradox is the starting point of my doctoral research.

I am a PhD candidate at the Centre for the Study of Regional Development (CSRD), Jawaharlal Nehru University, supervised by Professor Milap Punia. My research examines spatial justice in Delhi's urban green infrastructure across three dimensions: who gets the benefits (distributive), who gets a say (procedural), and whose communities are even recognised as legitimate (recognition). My central argument is that misrecognition is foundational — when planning frameworks refuse to acknowledge informal settlements as legitimate urban communities, procedural exclusion and distributive deprivation follow structurally.

My work goes beyond green cover counts and heat metrics. I use geospatial modelling, critical master plan analysis (1962 to 2041), and governance fieldwork in urban villages and unauthorised colonies to examine how Delhi's greening agenda concentrates ecological benefits in planned areas while displacing environmental costs onto the urban poor.

I hold a UGC Senior Research Fellowship and have received competitive funding from Utrecht University, UvA Amsterdam, TU Delft, RWTH Aachen, and the Austrian Student Union. My doctoral research has directly shaped the Shahjahanpur City Heat Action Plan 2026, one of the few instances of PhD-level work translating into a published municipal policy instrument in India.

Download CV View Research

docs.title: Skills
docs.text:
Geospatial Analysis ArcGIS Pro, QGIS, Google Earth Engine, InVEST Urban Cooling Model, remote sensing, spatial regression, hotspot analysis

Research Methods Mixed-method design, governance fieldwork, master plan analysis, community interviews, participatory mapping

Writing and Communication Peer-reviewed book chapters, policy reports, conference presentations, public engagement

docs.title: Academic Mobility, Fellowships, Scholarships & Research Schools
docs.text:
My academic and research journey has been shaped through fellowships, scholarships, doctoral workshops, and summer schools across India and Europe. These experiences have expanded my engagement with spatial justice, urban political economy, sustainability transitions, complexity science, institutional transformation, and future-making.

Interactive map of academic mobility and research training experiences (click numbered markers for details).

docs.title: Futuring for Sustainability, Urban Futures Studio, Utrecht University (2026)
docs.text: The Futuring for Sustainability Summer School explored how societies imagine, negotiate, and construct environmental futures. The programme connected sustainability transitions with broader political questions concerning democratic decline, ideological contestation, and social transformation. Using futuring methodologies developed by the Urban Futures Studio, participants engaged with innovative approaches to future-making and transformative research. The school significantly expanded my understanding of futures thinking as both a research method and a tool for addressing contemporary sustainability challenges.

docs.title: Reworlding Spring School, Hasselt University (2026)
docs.text: The Reworlding Spring School examined the role of institutions in socio-environmental transitions through themes of power, institutional change, embodied governance, and more-than-human relations. Through keynote lectures, workshops, and collaborative discussions, participants explored how institutions can be reimagined and reshaped in response to ongoing ecological and social crises. The programme deepened my interest in governance and institutional transformation while introducing alternative perspectives on environmental change and collective action.

docs.title: Amsterdam Complexity School on Climate Change (2026)
docs.text: The Amsterdam Complexity School on Climate Change brought together early-career researchers, policymakers, NGOs, activists, and other stakeholders to address climate challenges using complexity science as a guiding framework. Through lectures, tutorials, collaborative projects, and interdisciplinary teamwork, participants explored systems thinking, network dynamics, and collective problem-solving approaches to climate governance. The programme expanded my methodological perspective and highlighted the value of complexity-based approaches for understanding socio-environmental systems.

docs.title: Urban Space in Tension Between Financialisation and the Right to the City, Vienna Doctoral School of Social Sciences (2025)
docs.text: Organised by the PhD Club Critical Approaches to Policy and Governance, this international doctoral workshop engaged with contemporary debates on urban financialisation, housing, urban citizenship, and the Right to the City. Discussions explored how contemporary urban development is increasingly shaped by financial markets, speculative investment, and changing governance arrangements. The workshop strengthened my engagement with critical urban theory and urban political economy while providing a platform for dialogue with doctoral researchers working on urban justice issues across different contexts.

docs.title: IGCS Fellowship – Regenerative Urban Futures, RWTH Aachen University (2024)
docs.text: The International Graduate Campus Scholarship (IGCS) fellowship exposed me to interdisciplinary perspectives on regenerative urban futures and sustainability transformations. The programme encouraged critical reflection on how cities can move beyond conventional sustainability approaches towards regenerative frameworks that integrate ecological restoration, resilience, and long-term socio-environmental well-being. It strengthened my understanding of urban transformation processes and broadened my engagement with international sustainability scholarship.

docs.title: Planning and Design for the Just City, TU Delft (2024)
docs.text: This intensive summer school explored the intersection of spatial planning, urban design, governance, and spatial justice. Working with researchers, policymakers, civil society organisations, and practitioners, participants examined how justice-oriented planning approaches can address sustainability transitions in complex urban environments. The programme provided an opportunity to apply theoretical debates on spatial justice to practical planning challenges in The Hague and significantly enriched the conceptual foundations of my doctoral research.

docs.title: Urban Fellowship, Indian Institute for Human Settlements (2018–2019)
docs.text: The Urban Fellowship at the Indian Institute for Human Settlements (IIHS) provided my first formal interdisciplinary training in urban studies. Through classroom instruction, field immersion, and applied project work, the programme introduced me to urban governance, infrastructure systems, ecology, planning, housing, and development practice. The fellowship played a foundational role in shaping my interest in cities and continues to influence my efforts to connect academic research with real-world policy and planning challenges.

docs.title: Summary of Fellowships, Scholarships and Research Schools
docs.text:
Fellowship / Programme Institution Year UFS Futuring for Sustainability Summer School (EUR 2,000) Utrecht University, Netherlands 2026 Reworlding Spring School Hasselt University, Belgium 2026 Amsterdam Complexity School on Climate Change – Travel Grant (EUR 800) UvA Institute for Advanced Study, Amsterdam 2026 Austrian Student Union Travel Grant (EUR 800) Vienna Doctoral School of Social Sciences, Austria 2025 IGCS Fellowship – Regenerative Urban Futures (EUR 2,000) RWTH Aachen University, Germany 2024 Planning and Design for the Just City Summer School Scholarship (EUR 500) TU Delft, Netherlands 2024 UGC Senior Research Fellowship Government of India 2021–Present Urban Fellowship Award Indian Institute for Human Settlements (IIHS), Bangalore 2018–2019

Collectively, these experiences have expanded the conceptual foundations of my doctoral research beyond questions of environmental distribution and green infrastructure provision. They have enabled me to engage with broader debates on spatial justice, urban political economy, sustainability transitions, institutional transformation, complexity science, and future-making. Together, these programmes have strengthened my ability to connect geospatial analysis with critical urban theory and policy-relevant research on just and sustainable urban futures.

docs.title: Doctoral Research
docs.text:
Socio-economic Embeddedness of Urban Green Spaces in Delhi: A Spatial Justice Framework

PhD in Geography, Centre for the Study of Regional Development (CSRD), Jawaharlal Nehru University Supervisor: Professor Milap Punia | 2021 – Present

Delhi is officially one of India's greenest cities. It is also a city where millions of residents in informal and unauthorised settlements live without meaningful access to green space, cooling shade, or clean urban environments. This paradox is the starting point of my doctoral research.

Spatial justice is the organising framework, asking not just whether environmental resources are distributed fairly (distributive justice), but who participates in decisions about urban environments (procedural justice), and whose communities and claims are recognised as legitimate within planning systems (recognition justice). My central argument is that misrecognition is foundational: when planning frameworks refuse to acknowledge informal settlements as legitimate urban communities, procedural exclusion and distributive deprivation follow structurally.

My work goes beyond green cover counts and heat metrics. Through geospatial modelling, critical master plan analysis (1962 to 2041), and governance fieldwork in urban villages and unauthorised colonies, I examine how Delhi's greening agenda concentrates ecological benefits in planned areas while displacing environmental costs onto the urban poor.

docs.title: Who Gets to Stay Cool?
docs.text:
Cooling deficit and compound vulnerability in Delhi's informal settlements. Uses the InVEST Urban Cooling Model combined with food insecurity data to identify zones where thermal deprivation and socioeconomic vulnerability overlap. The Northwest Delhi corridor emerges as the primary compound vulnerability zone.

Read more

docs.title: Caste, Space, and Green
docs.text:
Examining the relationship between caste geography and green space distribution across Delhi's enumeration blocks using Census 2011 data. Methods include Spearman correlation, spatial error regression, bivariate LISA, and Lorenz/Gini analysis.

Read more

docs.title: Dilli Dehat Project
docs.text:
Recognition justice case study at Deoli village (Sangam Vihar–Sainik Farm cluster), examining forest department enclosure, johad blockade, and the erasure of historically documented urban villages from official planning. In collaboration with independent researcher Puneet Singh Singhal.

Read more

docs.title: Theoretical Framework
docs.text: My research draws on Fraser's three-dimensional theory of justice (redistribution, recognition, representation), Baviskar's work on bourgeois environmentalism, Ghertner's rule by aesthetics, Bhan's postcolonial urbanism, and Swyngedouw's post-political urbanism. The empirical sites and the theoretical framework together argue that Delhi's environmental governance is not a failure of implementation but a structural outcome of whose urban future planning is designed to produce.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text:
A selection of research projects from my doctoral work on spatial justice and urban green infrastructure in Delhi.

  • Who Gets to Stay Cool?

    Spatial inequality in urban heat mitigation across Delhi. Combines InVEST Urban Cooling Model outputs with food insecurity data to map compound vulnerability zones.

    Read more →

  • Caste, Space, and Green

    Examining the relationship between caste geography and green space distribution across Delhi's enumeration blocks using Census 2011 data and spatial regression.

    Read more →

  • Dilli Dehat Project

    Recognition justice case study documenting forest enclosure, johad blockade, and planning erasure in Delhi's urban villages, in collaboration with Puneet Singh Singhal.

    Read more →

docs.title: Caste, Space, and Green
docs.text: Caste Geography and the Distribution of Urban Green Infrastructure in Delhi

docs.title: The Problem
docs.text: Urban green space research in Delhi — and in Indian cities more broadly — has largely avoided the question of caste. Distributive analyses focus on income, settlement type, or administrative classification. This project argues that caste geography is a structuring force in urban environmental inequality that existing frameworks systematically undercount.

docs.title: Approach
docs.text:
Using Census 2011 data at the enumeration block level, the study maps the relationship between Scheduled Caste population concentration and green space distribution across Delhi. NDVI derived from satellite imagery and per-capita green space estimates provide the environmental outcome variables.

Methods include Spearman rank correlation, Geographically Weighted Spatial Error Regression, bivariate LISA (Local Indicators of Spatial Association), and Lorenz curve and Gini coefficient analysis to assess the degree of distributional inequality.

The analysis is designed to distinguish between the effects of settlement type, income, and caste geography — asking whether caste concentration predicts green space deprivation independently of class-based explanations.

docs.title: Argument
docs.text:
The preliminary findings suggest that caste geography and green space deprivation are not simply co-located by coincidence. Historically, the spatial logic of caste-based residential segregation in Delhi has deposited Dalit and lower-caste communities in peripheral, low-amenity zones. Urban green infrastructure investment has followed — and reinforced — these pre-existing geographies rather than counteracting them.

This makes caste a recognition justice problem before it is a distributive one: planning frameworks that do not name caste cannot address caste-based deprivation, even when their stated goal is equitable green space distribution.

docs.title: Significance
docs.text: The paper contributes to a growing literature on environmental casteism in Indian cities while grounding it empirically at a scale — the enumeration block — that allows spatial precision rather than aggregate inference.

docs.title: Status
docs.text: Analysis ongoing. Part of the doctoral dissertation on spatial justice and urban green infrastructure in Delhi.

docs.title: Who Gets to Stay Cool?
docs.text:
Spatial Inequality in Urban Heat Mitigation across Delhi

Presented at the LivedHeat Symposium 2026, IIT Delhi and TU Delft, New Delhi

docs.title: The Problem
docs.text:
Delhi's urban heat island effect is well documented. What is far less examined is who bears the cost of it. Official narratives celebrate Delhi's green cover. What they do not account for is how that greenery — and the cooling it provides — is distributed across the city's radically unequal settlement geography.

This project asks a simple but politically charged question: who gets to stay cool in Delhi, and who does not?

docs.title: Approach
docs.text:
The study combines two empirical threads that urban heat research typically keeps separate: ecosystem service distribution and socioeconomic vulnerability.

The InVEST Urban Cooling Model was used to quantify the spatial distribution of heat mitigation benefits across Delhi's settlement typologies — planned colonies, unauthorised colonies, urban villages, JJ clusters, and regularised resettlements. Model outputs were combined with Fair Price Shop (FPS) beneficiary data as a proxy for food insecurity and economic marginalisation, using a multiplicative composite raster to identify zones of compound vulnerability.

Spatial autocorrelation (Moran's I) and Gi* hotspot analysis were used to identify statistically significant clusters of high cooling deficit coinciding with high vulnerability populations.

docs.title: Key Finding
docs.text:
Informal settlements face a systematic cooling deficit relative to planned areas. The Northwest Delhi corridor emerges as the primary compound vulnerability zone — where thermal deprivation and food insecurity overlap at scale, affecting millions of residents who have the least capacity to adapt privately (air conditioning, relocation) and the least political leverage to demand public intervention.

The finding reframes urban heat not as a climate problem with technical solutions but as a governance problem with distributional consequences.

docs.title: Significance
docs.text: This is not simply a paper about heat. It is an argument that the distribution of urban cooling is a justice issue — shaped by decades of planning decisions about which settlements receive green infrastructure investment, which receive regularisation, and which remain in legal and spatial limbo. Heat vulnerability in Delhi is not random. It is structured.

docs.title: Status
docs.text: Paper under development. Presented at LivedHeat Symposium 2026, IIT Delhi and TU Delft, New Delhi, May 2026.

docs.title: Dilli Dehat Project
docs.text:
Urban Villages, Sacred Geographies, and the Politics of Recognition in Delhi

In collaboration with independent researcher Puneet Singh Singhal

docs.title: The Problem
docs.text:
Delhi's urban villages — the lal dora settlements and revenue villages absorbed by the expanding city — occupy a paradoxical position in planning law. They are historically documented, legally recognised in revenue records, and inhabited by communities with generational ties to the land. Yet in practice, they are systematically excluded from the green city vision: their commons are encroached upon, their water bodies blocked, their forests enclosed, and their spatial claims erased from official planning maps.

This is not administrative oversight. It is recognition injustice — the refusal of planning systems to acknowledge certain communities as legitimate claimants to the urban environment.

docs.title: The Case: Deoli Village
docs.text:
The primary case study is Deoli village in the Sangam Vihar–Sainik Farm cluster in South Delhi. The site documents three overlapping processes of dispossession:

Forest Department Enclosure. Land historically used as commons by the village community has been enclosed by the Forest Department under conservation narratives, effectively converting community-managed green space into bureaucratically controlled territory inaccessible to residents.

Johad Blockade. The village johad — a traditional rainwater harvesting pond central to both ecological function and community identity — has been blocked and degraded through surrounding development, severing a centuries-old relationship between the community and its water commons.

Planning Erasure. Despite being documented in revenue records, Deoli and settlements like it are absent or misrepresented in Delhi's Master Plans, rendering them invisible to the planning gaze and ineligible for green infrastructure investment.

docs.title: Theoretical Contribution
docs.text: The Deoli case is used to develop the recognition justice argument at the heart of the doctoral thesis: that misrecognition is not a residual effect of distributive deprivation but its structural precondition. A community that does not exist in the planning imagination cannot receive procedural representation or distributive redress. The sequence runs from recognition to procedure to distribution — not the reverse.

docs.title: The Dilli Dehat Project
docs.text: This case study is part of a broader collaborative initiative with independent researcher Puneet Singh Singhal documenting Delhi's urban village commons and sacred geographies — the johads, groves, shrines, and commons that persist in the interstices of the planned city and that official Delhi systematically fails to see.

docs.title: Status
docs.text: Fieldwork ongoing. Case study under development as part of the doctoral dissertation.

        fill_opacity=0.7,

docs.title: Heat Risk Index of Shahjahanpur City, 2025
docs.text:
A composite GIS analysis mapping thermal stress and demographic concentration across the Municipal Corporation

Chapter 2 · PhD Thesis, CSRD, Jawaharlal Nehru University · 2025

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
This chapter develops a multi-layer GIS framework to map and quantify heat risk across Shahjahanpur City, a Tier-2 urban centre in northern Uttar Pradesh, during the peak summer season of April–May 2025. The study constructs a composite Heat Risk Index at 30-metre resolution by combining Land Surface Temperature derived from satellite imagery with built environment density and demographic proxy indicators — identifying where thermal stress and human concentration systematically overlap across the city.

The analysis is fundamentally citywide in scope. It maps the spatial distribution of heat risk across all wards of the Municipal Corporation, identifying seven distinct high-risk zones. A significant finding is the systematic spatial intersection of these zones with Labour Chowks — informal gathering points where daily wage workers congregate each morning. This co-location was not assumed but observed: it reflects deeper structural patterns linking urban morphology, informality, and thermal vulnerability.

The work was completed in approximately three months — without access to local population data, relying instead on modelled demographic proxies — and contributed directly to the Shahjahanpur City Heat Action Plan 2026.

docs.title: Study Area
docs.text:
Fig. 1 — Labour Chowks of Shahjahanpur City, 2025. Red triangles mark the four primary informal labour market locations used as reference points within the citywide analysis (Gadiyana Chungi, Lal Imli Chowk, Chota Chowk, Pakka Pul).

Shahjahanpur Municipal Corporation in north-central Uttar Pradesh forms the study boundary. Four Labour Chowks were mapped as contextual reference points — informal labour markets established along major transport corridors. These sites anchor the interpretation of the citywide risk surface rather than serving as the primary unit of analysis.

docs.title: Land Surface Temperature
docs.text:
Fig. 2 — Mean Land Surface Temperature, April–May 2025. Range: 35.2°C (blue) to 48.9°C (red). Source: Landsat 8/9 thermal infrared bands.

Mean LST was derived from Landsat 8/9 thermal infrared bands for April–May 2025. Values range from 35.2°C in vegetated and water-body fringe areas to 48.9°C in the densely built western urban core — a 13.7°C intra-city thermal range. A pronounced urban heat island is concentrated in the old city fabric, with the Garhi Nala corridor providing the most substantial cooling contrast.

docs.title: Built Environment Analysis
docs.text:
Fig. 3 — Building Density, 2025. Built-up area per 900 sq.m grid cell (range: 0–20,144 sq.m). Source: GHSL building footprints.

Built-up area density was quantified at 30-metre resolution from GHSL building footprint data. Values reach 20,144 sq.m per 900 sq.m in the historic urban core — near-total impervious surface coverage. The distribution closely mirrors the LST pattern, confirming dense built fabric as the primary amplifier of surface thermal stress.

docs.title: Heat Exposure Index
docs.text:
Fig. 4 — Heat Exposure Index, 2025. Normalised composite of LST and building density (range: 0.22–0.87). Blue = low exposure; red = high compound thermal stress.

The Heat Exposure Index is a normalised composite (0–1) combining min-max scaled LST and building density rasters. It captures the combined thermal and built environment load at each location. Values range from 0.22 in cooler peri-urban areas to 0.87 in the densest built-up zones.

Methodology

Exposure = 0.5 × Normalised(LST) + 0.5 × Normalised(Built Density). Both inputs rescaled to 0–1 using city-wide min-max normalisation before compositing.

docs.title: Heat Risk Index
docs.text:
Fig. 5 — Heat Risk Index, 2025. Composite risk surface (range: 0–0.52). Green: low risk (0.13); yellow: moderate (0.26); orange: high (0.39); red: very high (0.52).

The Heat Risk Index integrates physical exposure indicators with demographic proxies to identify where thermal stress and human concentration overlap. Values range from 0 in low-density peripheral areas to 0.52 in the dense south-central urban core.

docs.title: Hotspot Analysis
docs.text:
Fig. 6 — Heat Risk Index with seven identified high-risk zones (blue numerals 1–7). Zones 5 and 7 in the south-central core carry the highest composite scores.

Seven distinct high-risk zones were identified through spatial analysis of the Heat Risk Index surface. Zones 5 and 7 — in the dense south-central urban area — are the most critical, with peak composite risk scores (0.39–0.52) and the highest density of informal outdoor workers.

docs.title: Systematic Intersection with Labour Chowks
docs.text:
Fig. 7 — Labour Chowk locations overlaid on the Heat Risk Index. All four chowks fall within medium-to-high risk zones — a structural pattern, not a design assumption.

When Labour Chowk locations are overlaid on the Heat Risk Index surface, all four fall within medium-to-high risk zones. Chota Chowk and Pakka Pul, in the densest southern urban fabric, exhibit the highest composite risk. This co-location was observed, not assumed — it reflects the structural alignment between urban morphology and the geography of informal labour markets.

Labour Chowk Risk Zone Level Gadiyana Chungi 0.13–0.26 Low–Moderate Lal Imli Chowk 0.26–0.39 Moderate–High Chota Chowk 0.39–0.52 High–Very High Pakka Pul 0.39–0.52 High–Very High

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
The spatial distribution of heat risk in Shahjahanpur City is not random — it follows the contours of the built environment and systematically overlaps with locations where the city's most economically precarious workers concentrate daily.

  • Peak LST of 48.9°C recorded in the built-up commercial core; 13.7°C intra-city thermal range
  • Building density is the strongest correlate of both exposure and final heat risk score
  • Seven distinct high-risk zones identified across the municipal area
  • All four Labour Chowks fall within medium-to-high risk zones — a structural, not incidental, pattern

docs.title: Limitations
docs.text:
Data constraints

The demographic layer relied on modelled proxies rather than actual population enumeration — local ward-level population data was unavailable at the time of analysis. The physical exposure surface is robust, but weighting by human concentration carries uncertainty. The full analysis was completed in approximately three months without a permanent field presence. With Census 2027 enumeration now underway, future iterations can replace modelled estimates with actual enumeration data, sharpening targeted interventions considerably.

docs.title: Policy Impact
docs.text:
Recognised by UPSDMA

The Shahjahanpur City Heat Action Plan 2026 has been recognised by UPSDMA as one of two benchmark CHAPs in Uttar Pradesh, with other districts directed to model their revisions on it.

I contributed the spatial analysis — a composite Heat Risk Index combining physical exposure indicators and demographic proxies to identify where thermal stress and human concentration overlap — and advised on intervention locations to ensure resources reach those who need them most.

docs.text:
